basics.description: **Note:** Please refer to the following before installing the solution: • Review the solution [Release Notes](https://github.com/Azure/Azure-Sentinel/tree/master/Solutions/Intel471/ReleaseNotes.md) • There may be [known issues](https://aka.ms/sentinelsolutionsknownissues) pertaining to this Solution, please refer to them before installing. The Intel 471 solution for Microsoft Sentinel ingests malware indicators into the Log Analytics workspace and provides a curated set of community hunting queries to proactively hunt for threats in Microsoft Sentinel. **Hunting Queries:** 25, **Playbooks:** 2 [Learn more about Microsoft Sentinel](https://aka.ms/azuresentinel) | [Learn more about Solutions](https://aka.ms/azuresentinelsolutionsdoc)

metadata.hidden: Hiding location, we get it from the log analytics workspace

basics.name: getLAWorkspace
basics.toolTip: This filters by workspaces that exist in the Resource Group selected

basics.name: workspace
basics.label: Workspace
basics.toolTip: This dropdown will list only workspace that exists in the Resource Group selected

options.text: This solution installs the following hunting queries. After installing the solution, run these hunting queries to hunt for threats in Manage solution view.

elements.name: huntingquery1
elements.label: AnyDesk Execution from Abnormal Folder - Potential Malicious Use of RMM Tool

options.text: Anydesk is a common and widely utilized tool for remotely controlling machines. However, it has also been adopted by many actors to remotely access victim machines and deploying malware or ransomware payloads. This Hunt Package is designed to exclude common paths where AnyDesk is executed from. Due to AnyDesk being able to be installed or executed from nearly any directory, Analysts should review whether AnyDesk is allowed, and follow internal policies if it is not authorized. Some common "red flag" type directories can be temporary directories, ProgramData and System32. Additionally, to appear more legitimate, some attackers may utilize installation paths that include legitimate sounding names, such as "Microsoft Management" or "Customer Service". This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery2
elements.label: AnyDesk Service Installation - Potentially Malicious RMM Tool Installation

options.text: Identifies when the AnyDesk service is installed onto a system. This can be legitimate if the organization allows AnyDesk, however if it is not a commonly utilized application, any service installations should be considered suspect. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents data connector (Event Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery3
elements.label: Autorun or ASEP Registry Key Modification

options.text: A common method that adversaries and malicious software alike achieve persistence is by adding a program to the startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key. Adding an entry to the "run keys" in the Registry or startup folder allows the referenced program to be executed when a user logs in. They are often utilized for legitimate purposes, however when utilized maliciously the key name/value is often obviously suspicious, such as random names, or objects loaded from temp or public folders. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery4
elements.label: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) Discovery

options.text: This Hunt Package identifies activity that could be associated with discovery behaviors targeting AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). This hunting query depends on AWS data connector (AWSCloudTrail Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery5
elements.label: Browser Spawning Suspicious Applications - Potential Exploit or Social Engineering

options.text: Exploitation of browsers often involves performing commands as a result of the exploitation. This Hunt Package focuses on commonly utilized scripting engines and other applications that can be abused from browser exploitation to retrieve, install or perform malicious commands. Additionally, browsers were found to be susceptible to a File Explorer attack, where a user is prompted to open a File Explorer window (commonly associated with uploading a file) and copy/paste a string into the File Explorer window. This action executes the command provided by the attacker. This technique was dubbed FileFix, which follows similar social engineering techniques to ClickFix. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery6
elements.label: Creating a Shadow Copy

options.text: This Threat Hunt package identifies suspicious activities related shadow copies being created from a variety of techniques. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery7
elements.label: Dump LSASS via Renamed procdump

options.text: Identify ProcDump usage as a means to dump LSASS data. ProcDump is a SysInternals tool that can be used to dump process memory, dumping the process memory of lsass.exe which can be used to obtain credentials. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery8
elements.label: Execution BAT Script to Unpack Payload

options.text: This Hunt Package is meant to identify a nuanced method of execution of a .bat file that can be indicative of an unpacking sequence that leads to the deployment of an additional executable. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery9
elements.label: File Created In Startup Folder

options.text: This package is designed to detect the activity around a file being created and put in the Windows Startup Folder. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery10
elements.label: Java Spawning Child Process by Unique Child Process Name - Potential Exploitation Activity

options.text: Identifies when Java spawns a child process and presents the data by most unique parent/child process first for the timeframe selected in the hunt query. The uniqueness of the child process can be an indication of unexpected activity being performed by Java, potentially by an exploit. The provided query is designed to return all java process executions, and therefore will return legitimate results. This is intended to catch the unknown child processes that may be unique to the attacker or target. The query will sort by uniqueness first and if supported by the tool, will limit unique counts returned, to help identify outliers. This package was originally inspired by the Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, and Text4Shell vulnerabilities released in late 2021 and 2022, respectively. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery11
elements.label: MeshAgent Suspicious Child Process - Potential Malicious RMM Tool Usage

options.text: Identifies the default installation process executing suspect applications, such as powershell, schtasks, cmd and other applications that can by abused by built in modules. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery12
elements.label: Methods for Downloading Files with PowerShell

options.text: This threat hunt package identifies instances where PowerShell is being used to download files from external sources, a common technique used in malware delivery and lateral movement. The hunt examines various methods by which PowerShell can be leveraged for file downloads, including the use of cmdlets such as Invoke-WebRequest (iwr), Invoke-RestMethod (irm), and Start-BitsTransfer (sbt), as well as direct utilization of .NET classes like System.Net.WebClient and HttpClient. The package also checks for potentially suspicious use of aliases (curl, wget) and other common executables that invoke PowerShell scripts to download malicious payloads. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery13
elements.label: NetSupport Manager Execution from Abnormal Folder - Potential Malicious Use of RMM Tool

options.text: NetSupport is a common and widely utilized tool for remotely controlling machines, and was even utilized as the base code for the NetSupport RAT. It has been adopted by many actors to remotely access victim machines and deploying additional malware or ransomware payloads. This Hunt Package is designed to exclude common paths where NetSupport is executed from. Due to NetSupport being able to be installed or executed from nearly any directory, Analysts should review whether NetSupport is allowed, and follow internal policies if it is not authorized. Some common "red flag" type directories can be temporary directories, Temp and System32. Additionally, to appear more legitimate, some attackers may utilize installation paths that include legitimate sounding names, such as "Microsoft Management" or "Customer Service". This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery14
elements.label: Potential Maldoc Execution Chain Observed

options.text: Detect the aftermath of a successfully delivered and executed maldoc (Microsoft Office). A detection indicates an Office document was opened from an email or download/link, spawned a suspicious execution, and attempted to execute code via common Windows binaries (i.e. powershell, cmd, rundll32, etc). This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery15
elements.label: PowerShell Encoded Command Execution

options.text:
Looks for valid variations of the -EncodedCommand parameter. This is commonly used to encode or obfuscate commands, and not all occurrences are malicious. For example, benign complex commands may require encoding to properly run on a target system. Analysis of the encoded command by base64 decoding the encoded data will be necessary. 

NOTE: When excessive results are received, notes provided in the Deployment Steps may be able to aid in tuning the dataset, per tool. Some general suggestions may be to group by parent processes to identify common, approved and legitimate, applications; group by common line and only review command lines observed less than 3 times, across 3 hosts (indicating single execution across multiple hosts); or limit the results by distinct number of hosts and event counts, such as limiting to less than 5 observed hosts or total execution count. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents MicrosoftThreatProtection data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent DeviceEvents Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery16
elements.label: Powershell History Modification or Deletion

options.text: Adversaries may attempt to clear the command history of compromised hosts to conceal the actions that they have performed during an attack. This content will detect if PowerShell's history was cleared, disabled, or modified. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery17
elements.label: Python Executing from Non-Standard Directory

options.text: This Threat Hunt package identifies suspicious Python executions originating from non-standard directories, such as hidden or unconventional locations signaling potential malware infection. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery18
elements.label: Remote Atera Agent Download - Command Line

options.text: This Threat Hunt package identifies when a tool like a lolbin is used to fetch the Atera's Remote Montoring and Management (RMM) agent directly from Atera's distribution domain. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery19
elements.label: Scheduled Task Executing from Abnormal Location

options.text: This hunt package is designed to capture activity associated with a scheduled task which includes abnormal locations in its details for execution. This is often a mark of persistence or malicious tasks created by malware or attackers. details. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery20
elements.label: Shadow Copies Deletion Using Operating Systems Utilities

options.text: Ransomware is known to delete Windows shadow copies before it begins encrypting the data on the victim host. This tactic is typically carried out with powershell, vssadmin or wmic. This package identifies activity by powershell, wmic, vssadmin or vssvc with command line arguments containing delete and variations of shadow. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery21
elements.label: Suspect Child Process to IIS Worker Process (w3wp.exe) - Potential Exploitation

options.text: This package is intended to identify abnormal child processes to the Microsoft IIS Worker Process (w3wp.exe). The w3wp process runs Web applications, and is responsible for handling requests sent to a Web Server for a specific application pool. Child processes such as cmd.exe or powershell.exe can be an indication of unexpected activity by the process as the result of an exploit attempt. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery22
elements.label: Suspicious Child Process for Java - Potential Exploitation Activity

options.text: Identifies when Java spawns unusual child processes, which can be an indication of exploitation of the Java process. Although Java may not be the target process of an exploit, it can be coaxed into executing malicious code as the result of an exploit in another service, such as Log4j or Spring-Core. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery23
elements.label: User Added to Default Privileged Windows Security Groups

options.text: This package is designed to capture the activity surrounding the execution of command line arguments that add a user to default privileged Windows Security Groups (local and domain). This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery24
elements.label: Wevtutil Cleared Log

options.text: This use case is designed to detect when "wevtutil" is used to clear logs to potentially obfuscate malicious events occurring prior to the clean up. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)

elements.name: huntingquery25
elements.label: WMIC Windows Internal Discovery and Enumeration

options.text: This will identify the potentially malicious use of WMI (Windows Management Interface) utilized for local enumeration and discovery of a host. This hunting query depends on SecurityEvents WindowsSecurityEvents data connector (SecurityEvent SecurityEvent Parser or Table)
This solution installs the following Playbook templates. After installing the solution, playbooks can be managed in the Manage solution view.
